target: New WIN-068 and WIN-069 entries in data/wins.json

WIN_068.finding: Kim & Chang 2018 confirmed eclipse geomagnetic effects — standard ionospheric Sq mechanism (Chapman 1933)
WIN_068.detail_claim: Ensemble analysis of 207 geomagnetic manifestations from 100 INTERMAGNET observatories across 39 solar eclipses (1991–2016) shows statistically significant magnetic field changes during eclipses. The dome model claims this confirms 'firmament-plate dielectric boundary shading' via the pressurized toroidal cavity's induction gradient.
WIN_068.detail_evidence:
The underlying data is real and well-documented. Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054) conducted a rigorous statistical analysis of geomagnetic field variations during 39 solar eclipses using INTERMAGNET data. They confirmed systematic patterns: Y-component increases, X/Z/F decreases, strongest at magnetic latitudes 30°–50°, over ~180-minute windows centered on maximum eclipse. This is genuine, peer-reviewed science.

However, the mechanism has been well understood since Chapman (1933). During a solar eclipse, the Moon's shadow reduces photoionization in the E-region ionosphere (90–150 km altitude), decreasing electron density and therefore electrical conductivity. This weakens the solar-quiet (Sq) dynamo current system — large-scale ionospheric currents driven by tidal winds crossing the geomagnetic field. The reduction in Sq current produces the observed ground-level magnetic variations. The Ashour–Chapman conductivity model quantitatively predicts the magnitude, timing, and component-dependence of the magnetic signature when fed with measured total electron content depletion.

The dome model's claim reframes this as 'firmament-plate dielectric boundary shading' — a relabeling of standard ionospheric physics. Critically, the dome offers no derivation showing how its cavity geometry produces the specific component patterns (Y increase vs. X/Z/F decrease), the latitude dependence (30°–50° maximum), or the 180-minute timescale. The standard model derives all three from the geometry of the Sq current vortex and its perturbation by the eclipse shadow. The dome merely asserts its cavity would produce 'transient ΔB' without specifying magnitude or direction.

The dome's own 'honest caveat' concedes that 'Globe has a competing ionospheric conductivity mechanism' and that 'Tier 1 is consistent with both models.' This is an admission that WIN-068 is non-discriminating — it does not distinguish the dome from standard physics. The dome then claims the discriminating test is 'contact-phase timing (C1/C4, not totality peak),' but Kim & Chang's data actually show maximum effect near eclipse maximum, not at contact phases — directly contradicting the dome's claimed discriminator.

This WIN duplicates the observational basis of WIN-010 (BOU 2017 eclipse, −10.9 nT) and WIN-025 (2024 9-station confirmation). All three cite the same physical phenomenon — eclipse-induced geomagnetic perturbation — just at different scales: single-station (WIN-010), 9-station (WIN-025), and 39-eclipse ensemble (WIN-068). Counting the same observation at three different aggregation levels as three separate 'confirmed predictions' inflates the WIN count.
WIN_068.detail_verdict_text: The eclipse geomagnetic effect has been explained by ionospheric conductivity reduction (Chapman mechanism) since 1933 — 93 years before the dome model claimed it. Kim & Chang 2018 is real peer-reviewed science, but it confirms standard ionospheric physics, not dome cavity dynamics. The dome concedes this is non-discriminating at 'Tier 1.' The WIN triples-counts the same phenomenon already counted in WIN-010 and WIN-025.
WIN_068.kernel_of_truth: The Kim & Chang 2018 data is excellent science. Solar eclipses genuinely do produce measurable, systematic geomagnetic variations. The dome model correctly identifies the phenomenon. The ensemble approach (39 eclipses, 100 stations) is methodologically superior to single-event studies. The dome's intellectual honesty in acknowledging the competing ionospheric explanation is noteworthy.
WIN_068.why_kernel_doesnt_save_claim: The mechanism is 93 years old. Chapman proposed the ionospheric conductivity explanation in 1933, long before this dome model existed. The Ashour-Chapman model quantitatively predicts the observations from first principles (electron density → conductivity tensor → Sq current perturbation → surface magnetic field). The dome model provides no competing derivation — it asserts 'firmament shading' causes 'transient ΔB' without computing the magnitude, direction, or timing. A domain where the standard explanation predates the dome's by nearly a century and the dome's own text concedes non-discrimination is the definition of 'Std Model Explains.'

WIN_069.finding: 6.2% RMSE is 10,000× worse than geodetic standards; AGD66→GDA94 shift is routine datum improvement, not evidence of topology
WIN_069.detail_claim: The V13 two-zone topology with aetheric refraction predicts that local straight southern controls remain exact while long transcontinental Australian routes expand beyond globe great-circle predictions. 12 roads and 9 cross-links yield 6.2% cross-equatorial RMSE. The AGD66→GDA94 datum shift is claimed as evidence of non-geocentric topology.
WIN_069.detail_evidence:
The kernel of truth: Australian road distances do exceed great-circle (geodesic) distances. This is expected — roads go around mountains, follow valleys, and deviate for terrain. The Nullarbor Highway's ratio of 1.00 (road distance ≈ geodesic distance) reflects the Nullarbor Plain being famously flat and straight — it's a geographic fact, not a dome prediction. The systematic excess on longer routes is a well-known property of road networks: longer routes accumulate more deviations from the geodesic. The dome model claims its V13 topology 'predicts' this excess, but any model that adds positive perturbations to distances will match the pattern.

The 6.2% cross-equatorial RMSE and 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error are presented as validation, but by geodetic standards they are catastrophic. Modern geodesy (using GDA2020, itself built on the geocentric WGS84/ITRF framework) achieves millimeter-level accuracy in positioning and sub-meter accuracy in distance. The dome model's best evidence — its Tier 1 in the 'Steelman Evidence Ladder' — has errors 10,000× larger than standard geodesy. If this is the strongest evidence, it reveals how weak the evidence base is.

The AGD66→GDA94 argument fundamentally misunderstands geodetic datum evolution. AGD66 was non-geocentric by design: it used the Australian National Spheroid fitted to local control points at Johnston Origin, with its center offset from the Earth's center of mass. This was standard practice for regional datums worldwide in the 1960s (NAD27 in North America, ED50 in Europe — all non-geocentric). The ~200 m shift to GDA94 was not a 'correction' revealing hidden topology — it was the predictable result of transitioning from a regionally-optimized datum to a globally-consistent geocentric one, enabled by satellite positioning technology (GPS/VLBI/SLR) that did not exist in 1966. Every continent underwent the same type of shift. The 'northeast' direction of the shift in Australia reflects the specific offset of the ANS ellipsoid from the geocenter, not some property of dome geometry.

The dome claims 'the globe needs datum resets and vertical patches where the ovoid cavity geometry predicts the expansion directly.' This inverts reality: datum improvements are refinements that improve accuracy from meters to millimeters. The dome model, by contrast, achieves 6.2% errors — that's errors measured in hundreds of kilometers on transcontinental routes. The globe framework doesn't need the dome's 'prediction'; it achieves accuracy the dome cannot approach.

Finally, the V13 prediction uses parameters calibrated against globe-framework distances. The disc radius a = 20,015 km = π × R_Earth, and the refraction profile n(r) contains H(r) derived from globe-calibrated measurements. The 'prediction' is circular: dome parameters are set to approximately reproduce globe distances, then the approximate reproduction is claimed as confirmation.
WIN_069.detail_verdict_text: The 6.2% RMSE is 10,000× worse than standard geodetic accuracy — making this the dome's strongest evidence also its most damning comparison. Road distances exceed geodesics for geographic reasons (terrain), not topology. The AGD66→GDA94 datum shift is a routine improvement from regional to global reference frames, identical to transitions on every continent. The dome's V13 parameters are calibrated against globe distances (a = π×R_Earth), making the 'validation' circular.
WIN_069.kernel_of_truth: Australian roads ARE longer than great-circle distances, and the Nullarbor IS notably straight. The AGD66-to-GDA94 transition DID involve a measurable coordinate shift. These are real geographic and geodetic facts. The dome model's presentation is methodologically aware — it uses official data sources, computes actual RMSE values, and acknowledges the Nullarbor as a control. The road-scaffold approach is a creative attempt at falsifiable evidence.
WIN_069.why_kernel_doesnt_save_claim: Road-to-geodesic ratios exceeding 1.0 are expected from terrain and routing, not topology. The 6.2% RMSE would be considered a failed model in any geodetic context. The AGD66 datum was intentionally non-geocentric (as were all 1960s regional datums globally) — the shift to geocentric GDA94 was a technology-driven improvement, not evidence of flat-earth geometry. And the V13 parameters that generate the 'prediction' are themselves derived from globe-framework distances (disc radius = π×R_Earth), so the agreement is circular.

holes_addressed: Coverage gap: our review had 67 WINs vs dome's 69
WIN-069 geodetic accuracy comparison (6.2% vs mm-level) quantified
AGD66 non-geocentric nature explained as routine 1960s practice
Circularity of V13 parameters (a = π×R_Earth) noted for WIN-069
new_evidence_added: Kim & Chang 2018 paper verification — real, peer-reviewed
AGD66/GDA94 datum history from Geoscience Australia sources
Quantified accuracy gap: 6.2% vs sub-mm geodesy (10,000× factor)

WIN_068: Objection: 'Contact-phase timing discriminates dome from globe.' Response: Kim & Chang's data shows peak effect at eclipse maximum, not at contact phases — contradicting the dome's discriminator.

Objection: 'The gravity coupling (Tier 3) is the real test.' Response: This is goalpost-moving. WIN-068 claims CONFIRMATION, not 'pending test.' If the confirmation requires a future test to distinguish models, it's not a confirmation.
Objection: 'Ensemble scale makes this different from WIN-010/025.' Response: Aggregation level doesn't create a new physical phenomenon. All three WINs cite eclipse magnetic perturbation.
WIN_069: Objection: 'The V13 two-zone topology predicts both local control AND long-route excess.' Response: Any model with distance-dependent positive perturbation predicts this. The globe + terrain does too, with 10,000× better accuracy.

Objection: 'AGD66 being non-geocentric proves something is wrong with the globe model.' Response: AGD66 was non-geocentric by design. So were NAD27, ED50, and every other 1960s regional datum. The transition to geocentric improved accuracy — the opposite of what 'broken topology' would do.
Objection: 'The 0.8% error on Sydney–Perth is good.' Response: Cherry-picking the best individual route. The aggregate metrics (6.2% and 10.2%) tell the real story.
